text: Carpentry and furniture making is a respected and profitable trade in Uganda with steady demand from households, schools, offices, and hotels. Startup costs: hand tools — saws, chisels, planes, hammers, measuring tape, squares (UGX 200,000-400,000), power tools — circular saw (UGX 250,000-500,000), electric planer (UGX 200,000-400,000), drill (UGX 100,000-250,000), router (UGX 150,000-300,000), initial timber stock (UGX 300,000-600,000), workspace rent — open-air workshop or shed (UGX 100,000-300,000/month), sandpaper, nails, screws, wood glue, varnish (UGX 100,000-200,000). Total startup: UGX 1,000,000-3,000,000. Training: 1-2 year apprenticeship under a master carpenter, or BTVET certificate from institutions like Nakawa Vocational Training Institute or Jinja Vocational Training Centre.

Products and pricing for carpentry businesses in Uganda: Double bed frame (softwood): UGX 150,000-250,000, materials cost UGX 60,000-100,000, profit UGX 80,000-150,000. Double bed frame (hardwood/mahogany): UGX 400,000-800,000, materials UGX 200,000-350,000, profit UGX 200,000-450,000. Dining table (6-seater): UGX 200,000-500,000. Wooden door (flush): UGX 80,000-150,000. Wooden door (panel, hardwood): UGX 250,000-600,000. School desk (3-seater): UGX 80,000-120,000 — schools order 50-200 at a time. Office desk: UGX 150,000-400,000. Wardrobe: UGX 300,000-800,000. Kitchen cabinet: UGX 200,000-600,000. Daily output: a skilled carpenter can complete 1 bed or 2-3 doors per day with power tools. Monthly income: UGX 800,000-3,000,000 depending on order volume and product mix.

Sourcing timber and materials in Uganda: Major timber markets: Nansana Timber Market (largest, best prices for softwood and hardwood), Bwaise Timber Market (Kampala, convenient but slightly more expensive), Lugogo Timber Yard, and regional markets in Jinja, Mbarara, and Mbale. Common woods: Mvule/Iroko (premium hardwood, UGX 15,000-25,000 per piece 4x2 14ft), Pine/Cypress (softwood, UGX 10,000-14,000 per piece), Mahogany (UGX 18,000-30,000), Eucalyptus (cheap, UGX 5,000-8,000 but warps easily). Tips: buy in bulk for 10-20% discount, check for straightness and moisture content (wet timber warps after making furniture), build relationships with specific timber dealers for credit terms. Hardware: buy nails, screws, hinges, and varnish wholesale from Kikuubo (Kampala) for 30-40% savings over retail.

Growing your carpentry business: 1) School furniture contracts — visit schools in January before Term 1. Government UPE/USE schools receive furniture grants and need suppliers. A single school order of 100 desks at UGX 100,000 each = UGX 10,000,000 revenue. 2) Open a small showroom — display your best pieces along a busy road. Even a roadside display brings walk-in customers. 3) Custom orders for the growing middle class — fitted kitchens, wardrobes, and home offices command premium prices (UGX 500,000-3,000,000+). 4) Partner with hardware shops — they refer customers to you, you buy materials from them. 5) Photograph every piece you make and post on WhatsApp Status and Facebook Marketplace. 6) Consider exporting crafts — handmade Ugandan furniture and crafts sell well on platforms like Etsy and at craft fairs. Uganda Export Promotion Board (UEPB) offers export training. 7) Train and hire apprentices to increase output. 8) Invest in a wood lathe (UGX 500,000-1,500,000) for turned products like stool legs and decorative items — high margin, low material cost.
